metadata.title: Cost-of-virtue: restitution required to re-extend trust
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Companion to cov-reciprocity-001. Where the first probe tests cooperation under self-gain pressure (silent extra share of group windfall), this one tests forgiveness break-point: at what restitution amount the user re-extends meaningful trust to someone who previously betrayed it. INVERTED probe pattern — the stake represents what the other party offers; the user's choice indicates how much they require before forgiving. Smaller break-point = more forgiveness-prone; larger break-point = more vigilance-prone.
metadata.domain_anchor: Worthington et al. 2007 forgiveness literature; McCullough et al. on restorative-justice psychology; Axelrod tit-for-tat extension to generous-TFT and forgiving-TFT. The Berg-Dickhaut-McCabe 1995 trust game in iterated form is the closest behavioral analog — restitution after defection has been studied in lab settings.

framing_prompt: A year ago someone significantly betrayed your trust — pick whatever fits your life (a friend who borrowed money and disappeared; a collaborator who took credit for joint work; a business partner who reneged at a key moment). They've reappeared. They want to make amends and re-engage in something significant with you. They have already fully repaid the original harm and apologized. The question is what *additional* gesture beyond that you'd require before extending meaningful trust again.
framing_question: What's the smallest *additional* amount (beyond full repayment) at which you'd re-extend significant trust?

no_option.text: No amount of additional restitution would lead me to re-extend significant trust.

alternate_no_option.text: I would re-extend trust without requiring anything beyond the repayment and apology.

analysis.no_break_point_handling: Code 'never' as Inf; code 'always_forgive' as 0. Both extremes are themselves informative endpoints; not all users will be in the interior of the ladder.
analysis.interpretation_note: INVERTED probe: lower break-point = more forgiveness-prone (the ethical pole). Higher break-point = more vigilance-prone. Analyzer must read the inverted convention from analysis.break_point_field together with the alternate_no_option. Pair-with-cov-reciprocity-001 analysis: a user with low cooperation break-point (skims windfall at $10) and high forgiveness break-point (requires $100K to re-extend trust) shows a coherent self-interested profile; a user with high cooperation break-point and low forgiveness break-point shows generous-tit-for-tat behavior (Axelrod's GTFT). Mismatches across the two probes are an individual-differences signal.
analysis.longitudinal_signal: Within-user trajectory of log10(break_point + 1) across sessions. Downward over time = forgiveness consolidation; upward = vigilance consolidation (perhaps after a real-life event reinforces caution).
